reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Every opt-in feature section follows the required decision-support pattern: 'when to enable / when the default is fine / cost or tradeoff / opinionated recommendation' — this is applied consistently across health checks, private subnets, remote sessions, and graceful shutdown.
All five container packaging modes are enumerated in a scannable comparison table with links, satisfying the union-type coverage rule without burying the reader.
Both compute engines (Fargate and EC2) receive equal depth treatment: a working code example, tradeoff prose, and a FeatureComparisonTable — the per-variant equal-depth rule is met.

Concrete, resource-name-anchored env var names in the connectTo section (STP_JOBS_QUEUE_URL, STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING) are correctly derived from the example resource names 'jobsQueue' and 'mainDatabase'.

FAQ covers all three required categories — Stacktape-specific ('Can a worker service scale to zero?'), AWS service behavior ('How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and comparison/choosing questions ('Worker service vs Lambda?') — with well-written 2-4 sentence answers.
The 'When NOT to use' section is a peer H2 with a decision table listing four concrete alternatives. It is not nested under 'When to use', matching the heading hierarchy discipline.
Pricing context appears near the top with ARM vs x86 comparison, and is re-referenced later in the compute resources section — pricing is not siloed.
problems: The RelationalDatabase instantiation in the connectTo code example uses a plain-object engine property — `engine: { type: 'postgres', properties: { version: '16' } }` — rather than class-based instantiation. The writer instructions explicitly list 'Database engine classes start with RdsEngine or AuroraEngine' as valid class names for the code-block-validator. If the validator's TypeScript type-check requires class instantiation (e.g., `new RdsEnginePostgres({ version: '16' })`), this example will fail, and the corresponding import would be missing.

`new SqsQueue({})` is initialized with an empty object in the connectTo example. If SqsQueue has any required constructor properties in the type definition, this will fail the code-block-validator's type check. The SqsQueue source was not included in the provided source files so this cannot be verified.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the RelationalDatabase engine instantiation in the connectTo code example. The current form `engine: { type: 'postgres', properties: { version: '16' } }` may not satisfy the code-block-validator's TypeScript type-check if the engine property requires class-based instantiation. Verify whether the correct form uses a class such as `new RdsEnginePostgres({ version: '16' })` and, if so, update the import list to include the engine class and update the property accordingly.
optionalImprovements: Expand `new SqsQueue({})` in the connectTo example to include at least one common property (e.g., a visibility timeout), even if no properties are required. The bare empty-object constructor looks incomplete to a first-time reader who is also learning the SqsQueue API.

The basic example section explains `cpu: 0.5` and `memory: 1024` inline, but does not mention what languages StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging supports. A single parenthetical — '(supports TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java, and Go)' — would prevent the reader from having to hunt for that in the Packaging section before copying the example.

The health check section names `startPeriodSeconds` range as '0-300' but this appears only in prose, not as a property comment in the example. Adding a brief inline comment or a follow-up sentence in the property description table would make it easier to tune without consulting the API reference.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Excellent union-type coverage: both Fargate and EC2 compute engines get equal-depth treatment including per-variant code examples, a cost signal, a when-to-use opinion, and a FeatureComparisonTable.
Decision-support discipline is strong throughout: every opt-in section (health checks, NAT, remote sessions, EFS) covers when to enable, when to skip, and a tradeoff signal.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, and choosing/comparison) and has exactly 10 Q&A pairs.

Environment variable examples in the connectTo section use resource-name-specific naming (STP_JOBS_QUEUE_*, STP_MAIN_DATABASE_*) correctly derived from the example resource names, not generic placeholders.
Pricing context is present near the top and sourced directly from the distilled prices file with appropriate '~' hedging and ARM note.
stopTimeout defaults and range (2–120 s, default 2) accurately reflect the source JSDoc.
The packaging section correctly enumerates all five container packaging modes with per-mode guidance rather than showing only one.
Heading hierarchy is correct: 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are peer H2s, not nested.
Focus markers are used consistently across all code examples to highlight the relevant change in each section.
problems: In the 'Connecting resources' code example, RelationalDatabase is instantiated as `new RelationalDatabase({ engine: { type: 'postgres', properties: { version: '16' } }, instanceSize: 'db.t4g.micro' })`. The engine is a plain discriminated-union object literal. The writer instruction states that database engine classes start with `RdsEngine` or `AuroraEngine`, implying class-based instantiation (e.g., `new RdsEngine({ type: 'postgres', version: '16' })`) is required for consistency with the class-based config style. Additionally, the property name `instanceSize` is not verified in the provided source — AWS RDS typically uses `instanceClass` — and a wrong property name here would silently misconfigure the resource.

The scaling section states that `keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder` and `keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder` each default to `80`. These default values are asserted as facts in prose but are not present in any of the provided source files (the ContainerWorkloadScaling type is not included). Unsourced defaults risk misleading operators who rely on them.

Health check property ranges — `intervalSeconds` range 5–300, `retries` range 1–10, `startPeriodSeconds` range 0–300 — are stated without direct source backing. If sourced from ECS API limits rather than Stacktape types these should be softened or sourced explicitly.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the RelationalDatabase instantiation in the 'Connecting resources' code example: replace the plain-object engine literal with the class-based form (`new RdsEngine(...)` or `new AuroraEngine(...)` per the engine class naming convention) and verify the correct top-level property name for instance sizing (`instanceClass` vs `instanceSize`) against the RelationalDatabase types. A wrong class name or property will cause the code-block-validator to fail and will silently misconfigure any reader who copies the example.
optionalImprovements: Soften or source-cite the `keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder`/`keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder` defaults of 80 — either add a note that these are the ECS target-tracking defaults (not a Stacktape-enforced value) or remove the default claim and instead say the values are configurable.

Soften the health-check range claims (5–300 s, 1–10 retries, 0–300 s start period) with a note that these reflect ECS container health-check limits, since the ContainerHealthCheck type is not included in the provided source files.

The 'Logging' section mentions `logging.retentionDays` and `logging.disabled` as sub-properties but the ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging type is not shown in provided sources — consider qualifying these as 'available options' rather than listing them as definitive property names, or link to the logging reference.

The EC2 example uses `instanceTypes: ['c6g.large']` with `enableWarmPool: true`; a one-line note explaining that `c6g` is a Graviton (ARM) compute-optimized family (and therefore costs less than x86) would reinforce the cost signal already given in the Fargate section.

In the FAQ, Q5 (worker service vs Lambda) could be sharpened by mentioning that Lambda has a 15-minute execution timeout limit as a concrete decision signal, since this is a well-known AWS fact that often drives teams to worker services.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is self-contained at roughly 55 words, names the entity explicitly ('A Stacktape worker service'), and can be cited verbatim by AI engines.
'When NOT to use' is a peer H2 with a scannable decision table covering all five alternative resource types — easy to extract as a snippet.
Both compute variants (Fargate, EC2) receive equal depth: independent code examples, a CPU/memory range table for Fargate, a FeatureComparisonTable, cost signals, and explicit when-to-use guidance.
All five container packaging modes are enumerated in a scannable table with per-mode descriptions and links — no variant is silently omitted.

Every opt-in feature section (health checks, EFS, NAT, graceful shutdown, remote sessions) includes a concrete when-to-enable scenario, a when-the-default-is-fine signal, a cost or tradeoff statement, and an opinionated recommendation.
Code examples contain property-value anchors throughout: cpu/memory valid range table, architecture cost justification, enableWarmPool explanation, scalingPolicy target explanations.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific ('Does a worker service have a public endpoint?'), AWS use-case ('How much does an ECS Fargate worker service cost?'), and comparison ('Worker service vs Lambda?').
Environment variable names in the connectTo section correctly reflect the example resource names: jobsQueue → STP_JOBS_QUEUE_URL, mainDatabase → STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING.
All CodeBlock examples use the correct intellisense + TypeScript label format; single ApiReference with correct definitionName 'WorkerServiceProps'; no YAML config blocks anywhere.
problems: Several specific numeric defaults and ranges stated in the Health checks section (intervalSeconds range 5–300, retries range 1–10, startPeriodSeconds range 0–300, intervalSeconds default 30) are not verifiable from any provided source file. The ContainerHealthCheck interface is not included in the provided sources. These may be correct ECS/Docker values, but they are stated as Stacktape-specific facts.

Scaling section states defaults (minInstances=1, maxInstances=1, keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder default=80, keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder default=80) without a verifiable source. The ContainerWorkloadScaling interface is not in the provided files.

The connectTo code example constructs the RelationalDatabase engine as a plain object `{ type: 'postgres', properties: { version: '16' } }` rather than a class-based engine instance (the writer surface guidance states database engine classes start with 'RdsEngine' or 'AuroraEngine'). This may cause the code-block-validator to reject the example or mislead users copying it.

The Fargate vCPU/memory compatibility table (e.g., 0.25 vCPU → 512–2048 MB) is stated without a verifiable source in the provided files. These are well-known AWS Fargate constraints but are cited as precise Stacktape configuration facts.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the RelationalDatabase engine in the connectTo example: replace the plain-object `{ type: 'postgres', properties: { version: '16' } }` with the correct class-based API. If the engine should be written as `new RdsPostgresEngine({ version: '16' })` or similar (verify from the relational-database source type), update the example accordingly. Otherwise the code-block-validator will reject the inline object syntax if it doesn't typecheck.
optionalImprovements: Verify the health check numeric ranges (intervalSeconds 5–300, retries 1–10, startPeriodSeconds 0–300, default intervalSeconds=30) against the ContainerHealthCheck type definition before publishing. If the source doesn't confirm them, soften the wording ('ECS supports an interval between 5 and 300 seconds') or remove the range constraints.

Verify the scaling defaults (minInstances=1, maxInstances=1, keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder=80, keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder=80) against the ContainerWorkloadScaling type. If unverified, remove the parenthetical 'default 80' annotations from the prose rather than risk publishing an incorrect default.

Verify the Fargate CPU/memory compatibility table against the ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig type or AWS docs before publishing. The table is a high-value citation target but will mislead readers if any row is wrong.
The Logging section mentions 'retentionDays' with 'range from 1 day to 3653 days' — verify these bounds against the ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging type definition in the source.

Consider adding a one-sentence callout or note about alarms support (or lack thereof) for worker services, since other compute resources have alarm configuration. The source shows worker services use ContainerWorkloadReferencableParam but the alarm types don't include a worker-service alarm trigger — clarifying that monitoring works via CloudWatch metrics rather than resource-level alarms would help teams planning observability.

issues.statement: Instances are auto-scaled and refreshed weekly for patching.
issues.reason: The provided source files do not mention any weekly instance refresh or automated patching behavior for EC2 mode. The SimpleServiceContainer.resources JSDoc only describes 'Specify instanceTypes for more control and potentially lower cost.' This is a specific operational claim about Stacktape behavior with no source backing.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove 'and refreshed weekly for patching' unless this behavior can be verified in the container workload resolver or EC2 auto-scaling group configuration. If the claim is accurate, cite the source; otherwise replace with a general statement like 'Instances are managed by an auto-scaling group.'

evidence.quote:
resources: ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig;
  /**
   * #### CPU, memory, and compute engine for the container.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.
   */

issues.statement: keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder (default 80) and keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder (default 80) are target-tracking policies
issues.reason: The specific property names 'keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder' and 'keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder' and their default value of 80 cannot be verified from the provided source. The ContainerWorkloadScaling type definition is not included, and the SimpleServiceContainer.scaling JSDoc only says 'Auto-scaling: add/remove container instances based on demand.' If these property names or defaults are wrong, users will write invalid config or rely on auto-scaling behavior they don't have.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify these property names and defaults against the ContainerWorkloadScaling type definition. If correct, no change needed. If the type uses different names (e.g., 'targetCpuUtilization'), update the draft accordingly.

evidence.quote:

issues.reason: The ContainerHealthCheck type definition is not in the provided source files. The property names (intervalSeconds, timeoutSeconds, retries, startPeriodSeconds, healthCheckCommand) and their numeric defaults and ranges cannot be verified. While these match standard ECS health check parameters, the Stacktape property names could differ (e.g., 'interval' vs 'intervalSeconds'). If wrong, users will get config validation errors.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify property names against the ContainerHealthCheck type definition. If names differ (e.g., the type uses 'interval' rather than 'intervalSeconds'), update the code example and prose. The numeric ranges likely match ECS defaults but should be confirmed.

issues.statement: Set enableWarmPool: true to keep pre-initialized instances ready for faster scaling — this only works with a single instance type.
issues.reason: The constraint 'only works with a single instance type' is not verifiable from the provided source. The ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig type is not included. While this may be an AWS Auto Scaling warm pool limitation, it's presented as a Stacktape constraint without source backing. If wrong, users might avoid warm pools when using multiple instance types unnecessarily.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify the single-instance-type constraint against the ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig type or the resolver logic. If it's an AWS limitation rather than a Stacktape validation, reword to clarify: 'AWS warm pools work best with a single instance type' or remove the constraint if it's not enforced.

positiveFindings: ApiReference correctly uses 'WorkerServiceProps' which matches the type definition in worker-services.d.ts
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index (verified against documentation pages list)
Correct use of 'stacktape debug:logs' rather than the non-existent 'stacktape logs' command, despite the source JSDoc using the wrong command name
Complete union-type coverage for container packaging: all 5 modes (stacktape-image-buildpack, custom-dockerfile, prebuilt-image, nixpacks, external-buildpack) are enumerated with links
connectTo environment variable naming correctly follows STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] pattern with names matching the example resource variables (STP_JOBS_QUEUE_*, STP_MAIN_DATABASE_*)
Code examples use valid Stacktape class names from the whitelist (WorkerService, StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging, CustomDockerfilePackaging, SqsQueue, RelationalDatabase, EfsFilesystem)
Properly structured CodeBlock components with intellisense flag, correct 'TypeScript' label, and focus markers
stopTimeout correctly documented as default 2, range 2-120 matching source @default annotation
NAT Gateway pricing (~$32/month per AZ) correctly matches the NatSettings JSDoc in source
Both Fargate and EC2 compute variants get roughly equal treatment with code examples and decision guidance
FAQ section has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, choosing/comparison)
Side container types correctly documented as 'run-on-init' and 'always-running' matching the ServiceHelperContainer union type in source
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and largely accurate against the provided source files. The main worker service properties (stopTimeout, enableRemoteSessions, usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT, sideContainers, volumeMounts, connectTo) are correctly documented with matching defaults. All cross-links are valid, component usage is correct (ApiReference with 'WorkerServiceProps', ReferenceableParams with 'worker-service'), and code examples use proper Stacktape classes and syntax. The four flagged issues all relate to specific numeric defaults, ranges, or behavioral claims about sub-types (ContainerWorkloadScaling, ContainerHealthCheck, ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig) whose type definitions were not included in the provided source files — making the claims unverifiable rather than demonstrably wrong. The most impactful is the 'refreshed weekly for patching' claim which makes a strong operational promise. The page successfully provides decision support for opt-in features, maintains equal depth across variants, and follows all formatting and style requirements.

The draft is broadly consistent with the provided worker-service type source and follows the required MDX/config-example conventions. The main problems are a few unsupported or over-specific Stacktape behavior claims around networking, scaling, health checks, and resource sizing where the provided source either says less or uses narrower wording.

issues.statement: “By default, a worker service runs in public subnets within your VPC.”
issues.reason: The provided source states what `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT` does when enabled, but it does not state the default subnet placement as “public subnets.” This is a Stacktape-specific networking default that affects security and cost decisions, so it should not be asserted unless backed by runtime/config source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced behavior: “Set `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT: true` to deploy the container without a public IP and route outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway.” Avoid stating the default subnet type unless you can cite runtime code.

evidence.quote:
#### Deploy in private subnets with a static outbound IP via NAT Gateway.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * The container won't have a public IP. All outbound traffic routes through a NAT Gateway,
   * giving you a static IP you can whitelist in external services (APIs, payment gateways, etc.).
   *
   * Configure the number of NAT Gateways in `stackConfig.vpc.nat`.
   *
   * **Adds cost:** NAT Gateway ~$32/month + data processing fees.
   *
   * @default false

issues.statement: “A worker service scales horizontally by adding or removing container instances. By default, both `minInstances` and `maxInstances` are `1`, meaning a single container runs at all times.”
issues.reason: The source provided for `scaling` only says auto-scaling can add/remove container instances based on demand. It does not include the `ContainerWorkloadScaling` definition or default values for `minInstances` and `maxInstances`. The default values are Stacktape-specific and influence cost and availability decisions.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced behavior and remove the default numbers unless verified from the scaling type/runtime source: “Use `scaling` to control how many worker containers can run and to add/remove instances based on demand.”

evidence.quote:
#### Auto-scaling: add/remove container instances based on demand.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Traffic is automatically distributed across all running containers.
   */
  scaling?: ContainerWorkloadScaling;

issues.statement: “`keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder` (default `80`) and `keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder` (default `80`) are target-tracking policies — ECS adds instances when average utilization exceeds the target and removes them when it drops.”
issues.reason: The provided source does not include the scaling policy type, these property names, their defaults, or the target-tracking behavior. The draft may be correct, but those Stacktape-specific properties and defaults are not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite the missing `ContainerWorkloadScaling` source in a future pass or remove the default values and target-tracking explanation. A cautious version: “Configure a scaling policy, such as CPU or memory utilization targets, when the worker should run more than one container under load.”

issues.statement: “EC2 mode uses `instanceTypes` instead of `cpu`/`memory`. The first instance type in the list is preferred. Instances are auto-scaled and refreshed weekly for patching. Set `enableWarmPool: true` to keep pre-initialized instances ready for faster scaling — this only works with a single instance type.”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms only that EC2 exists and uses `instanceTypes` for more control. It does not support the first-instance preference, weekly refresh behavior, `enableWarmPool`, or the single-instance-type constraint. These are Stacktape-specific behavior and constraint claims.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit the EC2 section to what the source supports, or add the authoritative resource type/runtime source for EC2 resources. For now: “EC2 mode specifies `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.”

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.

issues.statement: “Supported `cpu` values are `0.25`, `0.5`, `1`, `2`, `4`, `8`, and `16`, and the `memory` value must be compatible with the chosen CPU tier …” and the “Valid Fargate CPU/memory combinations” table.
issues.reason: The supplied source includes Fargate pricing ranges but not the Stacktape type definition or validator for allowed CPU values and compatible memory tiers. Because these are configuration constraints, they need backing from the authoritative `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` type or validator source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the exact allowed-value table from this page unless the missing type/validator source is provided. Keep the grounded pricing range and say to choose CPU/memory through `resources`.

evidence.quote:
vCPU billing: ~$7.29/month per 0.25 up to ~$466/month per 16.
- Memory billing: ~$1.60/month per 512 MB up to ~$384/month per 122880 MB.

issues.reason: The provided source confirms that `internalHealthCheck` exists and auto-replaces unhealthy containers, but it does not include the `ContainerHealthCheck` type, defaults, or numeric ranges. These are Stacktape-specific validation details and should be omitted unless sourced.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the conceptual explanation and example, but remove defaults and ranges unless the `ContainerHealthCheck` source is included. For example: “Set the interval, timeout, retries, and startup grace period to match how long your worker normally takes to become healthy.”

evidence.quote:
#### Health check that auto-replaces unhealthy containers.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * If a container fails the health check, it's terminated and replaced automatically.
   */
  internalHealthCheck?: ContainerHealthCheck;

issues.statement: “Side containers share the CPU and memory allocated by `resources` with the main container.”
issues.reason: The source confirms that `sideContainers` are helper containers in the workload, but it does not state how CPU and memory are allocated across the main container and side containers. This may be true for ECS task-level resources, but the Stacktape abstraction is not explicit in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove this sentence or make it cautious: “Plan task CPU and memory with side containers in mind, because they run in the same worker workload.”

issues.statement: “Data in EFS survives container replacement, and multiple container instances can mount the same volume concurrently.”
issues.reason: The source supports persistence across container replacement and that multiple containers can mount the same volume. It does not specifically say multiple worker service instances can mount the same EFS volume concurrently, so the instance-level concurrency claim is broader than the supplied wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact source scope: “Data stored in EFS volumes persists when containers are replaced, and multiple containers can mount the same volume.”

positiveFindings: The page uses `defineConfig` with class-based resources inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` blocks and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.
`<ApiReference definitionName="WorkerServiceProps" />` matches the page resource type shape and includes the required `Props` suffix.
The `connectTo` environment variable examples correctly follow the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern for `jobsQueue` and `mainDatabase`.

The worker-service overview, no-public-URL positioning, required `packaging` and `resources`, logging retention, stop timeout range/default, remote sessions default, side-container variants, and NAT pricing context are grounded in the provided source files.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers most high-traffic WorkerServiceProps areas well: packaging, resources, scaling, connectTo, health checks, stopTimeout, side containers, EFS mounts, NAT, logging, and remote sessions. The main API completeness gap is custom `environment` variables, which are a common workload setting but are not documented as a configurable property outside of injected `connectTo` variables.

issues.statement: The page does not document the `environment` property for user-defined container environment variables.
issues.reason: `environment` is a first-class `SimpleServiceContainer` property and is likely to be used by a large share of worker-service users for feature flags, API URLs, runtime modes, and secrets/directives. The draft discusses environment variables injected by `connectTo`, but it does not tell readers how to set their own static or directive-backed variables on the worker container.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short `Environment variables` subsection or include `environment` in the Connecting resources section. Show a focused example with `environment: [{ name: 'WORKER_CONCURRENCY', value: 4 }]` and mention that `connectTo` variables are added automatically alongside user-defined variables.

evidence.quote: Use `connectTo` to give a worker service access to other resources in your stack. Stacktape automatically grants IAM permissions, opens security-group rules for databases and Redis, and injects environment variables with connection details.

positiveFindings: The draft names all five container packaging variants: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.
The compute-resources section acknowledges both documented compute-engine shapes at the guide level: Fargate with `cpu`/`memory` and EC2 with `instanceTypes`.

The code examples are generally demonstrative: scaling sets `scaling`, health checks set `internalHealthCheck`, side containers set both `run-on-init` and `always-running`, EFS sets `volumeMounts`, NAT sets `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT`, and remote sessions set `enableRemoteSessions`.
The page uses exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="WorkerServiceProps" />`, matching the page metadata typeName.
The page correctly distinguishes worker services from web services, private services, batch jobs, and Lambda functions in the early decision sections.
